abstract.text: Understand how Ensembles resolves conflicts and how to customize the process.

inlineContent.text: When multiple devices change the same data concurrently, conflicts arise. Ensembles resolves these automatically using causal revision tracking, and provides delegate hooks for cases that need custom logic.

Every event carries a revision set that records which events from other peers have been seen. When events are replayed during a merge, the integrator determines the causal ordering: if one change “happened after” another (it had seen the other’s revision), that change wins. For truly concurrent changes to the same attribute, the event with the highest
(a monotonically increasing counter across the device’s event history) is used as a tiebreaker.
Conflict resolution operates at the
attribute level
: each attribute is resolved independently using last-writer-wins (LWW). For to-many relationships, Ensembles uses an
add-wins set
strategy — additions from both sides are preserved, and only explicit deletions are removed.
Ensembles provides
eventual consistency
: all devices that have seen the same set of events will converge to the same state, regardless of the order in which events were received.
This means that for most apps, conflicts are resolved transparently without any code on your part.
For cases where automatic resolution isn’t sufficient, the delegate provides a two-context pattern for inspecting and repairing merged data before it’s committed.
Before the merge save, the ensemble calls:
The
contains all the merged changes about to be committed. Use it to inspect what will happen:

code: // Check for constraint violations, business rule issues, etc.
return true // Return false to abort the merge entirely

inlineContent.text: Do not modify the
directly. Instead, make corrections in the

code: // Fix up objects here — these changes are captured
// as new events and synced to other devices
let item = reparationContext.object(with: objectID) as! Item

inlineContent.text: Changes made in the reparation context are captured as new events, so they propagate to all peers on the next merge.
The saving context and reparation context have a parent-child relationship. Do not nest
or
calls between them — this will deadlock.
If the merge save fails (e.g., due to a validation error), the ensemble calls:
Inspect the
to determine which objects caused the failure, make corrections in the
, and return
to reattempt the save. Return
to abort.
The reparation context operates on
and
, so it isn’t directly usable from SwiftData
types. The recommended approach for SwiftData is to design your model so that attribute-level merging always produces valid results. See
for specific strategies.
When the same logical object is created independently on multiple devices, automatic merging produces duplicates — two objects with different identifiers but the same meaning (e.g., two “Work” tags).
To handle this, implement the global identifier delegate method:

code: if let tag = object as? Tag {
return tag.name  // Use name as global ID
return nil  // Let Ensembles assign a random ID

inlineContent.text: When Ensembles encounters objects with matching global identifiers from different devices, it merges them into a single object rather than creating duplicates.
Global identifiers must be
immutable
— once assigned, an object’s identifier must never change. If you need to change an identifier, delete the object and create a new one.
In exceptional circumstances, the ensemble may be forced to detach:
The cloud identity changed (e.g., the user signed out of iCloud)
The hardware identity changed unexpectedly
Data corruption was detected (incomplete mandatory events)
The device’s registration was removed from the cloud
When this happens, the ensemble calls
. You should update your UI to reflect the detached state and can attempt to re-attach to resume syncing.

abstract.text: Invoked when the ensemble is forced to detach due to an unexpected issue.
